content:
# The Harbor at Low Tide

She came to the harbor every October, when the season had finished with itself.

The fishing boats sat lower now, their paint peeling in long curling strips like birch bark after a dry summer. She noticed how the dock planks had warped slightly upward at their ends, as though trying to remember some earlier shape. The iron cleats were orange with rust, each one a small monument to the patient work of salt air.

Her father had kept a boat here. She could still find the slip by counting — fourth from the harbormaster's shed, the one with the broken piling that leaned just slightly toward Portugal. The piling was still there, leaning a little further now. Everything was still there, only more so.

She sat on an overturned crate and watched a gull pull apart a crab on the nearest dock. The gull worked methodically, without urgency. The crab became smaller pieces, then smaller still, until the wind took the lightest parts away.

The water was the color of old pewter. A boy on the far dock threw a stone and the rings spread outward, thinning, until the harbor held them and they were gone.

She thought about her father's hands — how rough they had been, how certain. She pressed her own palms together and felt the warmth there, temporary and specific, the way all warmth is.

The tide was going out. It always was.
